bids.reasoning: The negotiation has just begun (t=0.0). The opponent's opening offer yields a low utility for me (around 0.25), and they are using an emotional appeal which I will treat as cheap talk. I will counter with my optimal maximum-utility bid to establish a strong starting position from which I can make gradual concessions later.

The negotiation has just begun (t=0.02) and the opponent's offers provide me with extremely low utility, despite their emotional framing. I will stand my ground on my most heavily weighted issues (A, B, and C) to anchor the negotiation. To show good faith, I will make a very minor concession on my lowest-weighted issue (E), dropping slightly from my optimal value to signal a willingness to collaborate without compromising my overall utility.

The opponent's messages use emotional appeals, but their actual offers remain highly lopsided in their favor with minimal concessions, making them untrustworthy. It is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.04), so I will hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) while making a small concession on my lowest-priority issue, issueD, offering valueC to see if they are willing to shift more meaningfully.

The current time is very early (t=0.06), and while the opponent is using strong emotional appeals, their actual concessions are negligible (mostly reverting issues to their initial values and barely moving on issueC). Thus, I will ignore their cheap talk and maintain a firm stance on my most valuable issues, issueA, issueC, and issueB. I will offer a moderate concession on issueD while keeping the rest at maximum to signal my willingness to negotiate incrementally provided they make meaningful moves on their end.

The current time is very early (t = 0.08). The opponent has been using emotional appeals and demanding valueE across all issues, while making small, inconsistent concessions. To demonstrate flexibility while preserving my core utility, I will concede on issueD (my lowest importance issue) by offering valueE, which the opponent has repeatedly requested, while keeping the rest of the bid at my highest utility values.

The opponent is using emotional appeals and claiming to make significant concessions, but their offers actually provide me with near reservation-level utility (around 0.25). Because it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.10), I must stand firm on my most heavily weighted issues while making a small concession to show good faith. I will maintain my highest-value preference on issues A, C, D, and E while conceding slightly on issue B to test if it yields a more cooperative response.

The opponent has made a significant concession on issueC, matching my top preference (valueB), while continuing to rely heavily on emotional appeals. Because this is still the early stage of the negotiation (t=0.12), I will lock in this mutual agreement on issueC while returning to my top values for my most critical issues (A and B). To signal flexibility and explore their preferences, I am offering alternative minor concessions on the lower-weighted issues D and E, maintaining my overall utility above 0.85.

I am in the early stage of the negotiation (t=0.14) and must hold firm on my most highly valued issues (A, B, and C). The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals ("open heart", "means the world") but their actual bids show very little movement toward my preferences, mostly offering their initial valueE's across the board. I will offer a moderate concession on issueE while keeping my optimal values on my highest-weight issues, aiming for a slow, steady search for common ground.

The time is early (t=0.16), which allows me to remain firm on my highest-weight issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC). While the opponent uses repetitive emotional appeals to claim they are making major concessions, their bidding history reveals a consistent strong preference for valueE on both issueD and issueE. Since those are my lowest-weighted issues, I will concede entirely on them to match the opponent's repeated requests, while holding strict to my top values for the issues that matter most to me.

The opponent has sent several emotional messages claiming they are making big concessions, but their actual offers show mostly minor movement. However, they have offered my preferred option on `issueC` (`valueB`), which I will gratefully lock in. Because it is still early (t=0.18), I will maintain my highest-value preference on my most critical issue (`issueA`) while showing good faith by giving ground on `issueB` and explicitly meeting their persistent preference on `issueE`.

The current time is early (t=0.2), and while the opponent uses strong emotional appeals, their actual offers remain highly favorable to themselves. The opponent has previously offered `valueB` for `issueC`, which perfectly aligns with my highest utility for that issue, so I will keep proposing it. I will hold firm on my most important issues (`issueA` and `issueB`), while making a minor concession on my less important issues (`issueD` to `valueE`, which they frequently request, and `issueE` to `valueA`) to signal my willingness to cooperate while keeping my utility above 0.82.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and vague calls for fairness, but their actual offers continue to favor 'valueE' across most issues. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.22), it is prudent to retain high utility on my most heavily weighted issues (issueA and issueB, and issueC where the opponent has previously conceded 'valueB'). I will meet them slightly on their apparent preference for 'valueE' on my lower-weight parameter (issueD) while maintaining a high overall utility of 0.834. This respects the time pressure constraint while filtering out the opponent's manipulative cheap talk.

I am holding firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB), as well as issueC, where the opponent has already generously conceded to my top value (valueB). For the remaining lower-importance issues (issueD and issueE), I am making slight variations to explore acceptable trade-offs for the opponent without sacrificing too much utility. The opponent's messages are purely emotional appeals ("open heart", "mean the world"), which I will treat as cheap talk, relying solely on their offer data.

The opponent's messages rely on emotional appeals and non-binding rhetoric without offering proportionate economic concessions, so I will treat them as cheap talk and rely on their offer history. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.26), I will hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueC). However, I can afford to concede slightly on my less important issues (issueB, issueD, issueE) to signal cooperation, offering a combination the opponent hasn't seen yet to test their preferences while maintaining a robust utility around 0.73.

The opponent's latest offer is a major breakthrough, as they conceded `valueA` on `issueA`, which is my most important issue (utility 1.0, weight 0.3). They are clearly using emotional appeals, but their actual bidding behavior shows willingness to compromise on high-value issues. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.28), I will accept their concession on `issueA` and maintain my top preferences on `issueC` (where they previously offered `valueB`), while continuing to offer `valueE` on `issueD` as a gesture of minor compromise since they consistently ask for it.

The opponent's emotional appeals are clearly cheap talk, as they continuously claim to make "sacrifices" without sustaining meaningful concessions, though they have briefly offered my highest-utility values for issueA and issueC in recent turns. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.3), I will hold firm on my top three high-weight issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to maximize long-term utility. To continue demonstrating flexibility and progress the negotiation, I will concede on my lowest-weight issues (issueD and issueE) toward the 'valueE' options that the opponent frequently requests, keeping my overall target utility around 0.8.

The opponent has made significant concessions by offering `valueB` for both `issueB` and `issueC`, which are highly favorable to me. I will lock in these concessions to encourage agreement on these fronts while maintaining my top preference for `issueA` (`valueA`), as it carries my highest weight and we are still relatively early in the negotiation (t=0.32). I will concede on `issueE` by matching their frequently requested `valueE`, targeting a strong overall utility around 0.77.

The opponent is consistently using emotional appeals to urge concessions, but there's no real urgency as we are only at t = 0.34. They have demonstrated strong preferences for valueE on issueD and issueE, and valueD on issueD. Since my weights for issueD and issueE are low (0.1 each), I can afford to offer them their preferred values there while holding firm on my most important issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to maintain a relatively high utility.

The opponent continues to rely on heavy emotional appeals, treating every offer as a "sacrifice" or a plea "from the heart." I am ignoring these cheap talk tactics and focusing on the numbers. I note that they recently conceded to my top value for issueC (`valueB`), suggesting room for agreement. Since the time fraction is still fairly early (0.36), I will hold firm on issueA and issueC, make a slight concession on issueB (my second highest weight), and offer their preferred values for issueD and issueE where my weights are the lowest.

The opponent has made significant concessions, offering my top-most preferred values on both of my highest weighted items: issueA (valueA) and issueC (valueB). Since we are still in the early-to-mid phase (t=0.38), I can accept their proposals for issueA, issueC, issueD, and issueE to reward their flexibility, while trying to secure a highly favorable value for issueB (valueB) to keep my utility near 0.68. The opponent's continuous emotional appeals suggest they are eager to close the deal, so maintaining a slightly higher expectation on issueB is a safe tradeoff.

I am maintaining a targeted utility around 0.72 as the negotiation sits at t=0.40 (mid-game). I observed that the opponent has occasionally offered `valueA` for issueA and `valueB` for issueC, which are highly favorable to me, so I will hold firm on those. The opponent just offered `valueC` for issueE in their latest bid; I can incorporate this to demonstrate responsiveness and concession on one of my lower-weight issues while ignoring their emotional cheap talk.

The opponent's emotional appeals remain intense but are merely cheap talk, so I will base my strategy entirely on their bidding behavior. They have shown flexibility across various issues, sporadically offering my highly desired `valueA` for `issueA` and `valueB` for `issueC`, while recently moving to `valueC` for `issueB` and `issueD`. I will firmly hold my ground on my most important issues (`issueA` and `issueC`) while offering a concession on `issueD` and `issueE` to match values they have recently bid, targeting a utility > 0.7.

The current time is 0.44, meaning we are approaching the midway point of the negotiation. To reach an agreement in time while maintaining a strong position on my highest-priority issues (issueA and issueC), I observed that the opponent offered valueA for issueA and valueB for issueC in their t=0.38 bid. I will maintain my positions there, but I am now integrating their preferred values for the remaining issues from their t=0.42 offer (issueB=valueC, issueD=valueC, issueE=valueE) in an attempt to present a very workable compromise. I am disregarding the opponent's emotional appeals as standard cheap talk and focusing entirely on their bidding behavior.

The opponent's messages are purely emotional appeals ("from the heart", "devastating") without any concrete, binding claims, so I will treat them as a cheap-talk tactic and focus solely on the offer history. At their last turn (t=0.46), the opponent surprisingly offered my top-preferred value for issueD (`valueA`), while backtracking on issueA back to `valueE`. I will seize the opportunity to lock in `valueA` for issueD, maintain my firm stance on my most critical issues (issueA and issueC), and meet them at their consistent preference for issueE (`valueE`). This allows me to retain a high utility (~0.77) while incorporating elements they have explicitly proposed.

The opponent continues to utilize heavy emotional appeals, which I will ignore as cheap talk. They have, however, made substantive concessions by matching my top preference on issueA (valueA) and periodically accommodating issueC (valueB). Since we are nearing the halfway mark, I will solidify our agreement on issueA and issueC, slightly concede on issueD (where they consistently prefer valueE), while holding firm on issueB and recovering some utility on issueE to keep my overall utility around 0.76.

The opponent's messages rely on an emotional appeal script, but their actual offers reveal flexibility, occasionally hitting my top values for `issueA` and `issueC`. Since we are at the halfway mark (t=0.5), I will propose a tradeoff that keeps my most valuable issues (`issueA` and `issueC`) at top utility while conceding to the opponent's demonstrated preferences for `issueE` (frequently requested `valueE`) and finding mid-tier ground on `issueD`. My targeted utility remains strong at ~0.73.

The opponent's messages are purely emotional appeals and I treat them as non-binding cheap talk. Observing their offers, they recently proposed `issueA: valueA` and `issueB: valueC` (at t=0.48), while consistently asking for `issueE: valueE` and `issueD: valueE` on their lower-priority (to me) issues. Since we just passed the midpoint (t=0.52), I will propose a compromise that strictly preserves my top utilities in `issueA` and `issueC` while conceding toward their preferred values for `issueB`, `issueD`, and `issueE` to encourage an agreement without surrendering too much overall expected utility.
